title: Reflection (Self-Reflection)
tldr: An LLM critiques its own output and revises it, catching errors at the cost of extra inference calls.
plain_english: Reflection is when you ask a model to read what it just wrote, spot problems, and fix them—like editing your own draft. The model isn't always good at this (if it can't do the task, it usually can't judge it either), but when it works, you get a second pass without hiring another model or person.
how_it_works: A reflection loop runs in three steps: (1) Generate a draft response. (2) Send that draft to a critique prompt asking the model to identify errors, gaps, or style issues. (3) Send the draft and critique to a revision prompt to fix the problems. The critique works best when grounded in external signals—test results, retrieved facts, rubric scores—rather than the model's own judgment alone. Variants like Reflexion add memory so the agent tracks past failures within a session and avoids repeating them.
why_it_matters: Reflection gives you a second look without deploying a second model. For code, plans, and technical writing, a single reflection pass catches 20–40% of errors a non-reflected first pass misses. This improves output quality without needing human review or ensemble models.
example: A coding agent generates a Python function for data validation. A critique step identifies a missing edge case (negative numbers). A revision step rewrites the function to handle it. Without reflection, the bug reaches the user; with it, the agent self-corrects before returning the final output.
pitfalls: A model's critique is only as reliable as its ability at the task itself—weak code writers make poor code reviewers.
Reflection doubles or triples latency; use it only when quality matters more than speed.
Grounding critique in rules or test outputs (not vague aesthetic judgment) is critical; otherwise the model revises in circles.
The model can fail at both critique and revision, introducing new errors while 'fixing' old ones.
when_use: Use reflection when you can afford extra inference time, the task has objective success criteria (tests, rubrics, fact checks), and quality is worth the latency penalty—especially for code, formal writing, and reasoning chains.
when_avoid: Avoid reflection in real-time applications (chatbots, streaming), tasks where the model itself is weak, or when latency is the bottleneck.

faq.q: Is self-reflection the same as chain-of-thought?
faq.a: No. Chain-of-thought is step-by-step reasoning within a single forward pass. Self-reflection is a separate critique-and-revise loop after the first output is complete. You can combine them.

faq.q: How much does reflection improve output quality?
faq.a: On code and structured tasks, expect 20–40% error reduction on tasks the model already partially understands. Results vary by domain and model capability.

faq.q: Does reflection work if the model is bad at the task?
faq.a: Usually no. If a model struggles to write code, it will also struggle to critique code. Reflection amplifies existing capability, not create it.

faq.q: What's the difference between reflection and using two models?
faq.a: Reflection uses one model twice (cheaper, slower); two-model ensemble uses different models (faster, more expensive, often more robust).

title: Regression (ML Task)
tldr: Predicting continuous numeric values—like house prices or temperature—using supervised learning.
plain_english: Regression is predicting a number, not a category. If classification answers "Will this customer churn?" (yes/no), regression answers "What is the customer's lifetime value?" (a dollar amount). You train on past examples and their numeric outputs, then apply the learned pattern to new inputs.
how_it_works: You provide labeled training data: inputs paired with numeric targets. The model learns a function mapping inputs to outputs by minimizing a loss function—typically MSE (mean squared error, sensitive to outliers), MAE (mean absolute error, robust), or Huber (hybrid). During inference, the model outputs a predicted number. Evaluation uses metrics like RMSE, MAE, or R² to measure how close predictions are to ground truth.
why_it_matters: Pricing, forecasting, ranking, and valuation models are regression problems. Errors compound silently in production—a regressor accurate on average but biased in certain input regions can make systematically wrong predictions for a customer subgroup without triggering alerts.
example: An e-commerce company trains a regression model on past orders to predict delivery time (in hours). Input features: item weight, destination zip code, time of day. Output: predicted delivery hours. In production, the model works well for urban areas but systematically underestimates rural deliveries, causing missed SLA targets for a subset of customers.
pitfalls: Confusing MSE (penalizes large errors heavily) with MAE (treats all errors equally)—pick based on whether outliers represent true anomalies or measurement noise.
Assuming your model is well-calibrated because average error is low; regional bias can hide in stratified subgroups.
Using regression metrics (R², RMSE) interchangeably without understanding their scale-dependence; RMSE in dollars differs from RMSE in percentages.
Training on past data with systematic bias (e.g., historical pricing skewed toward wealthy zip codes) and inheriting that bias in predictions.
when_use: Use regression when your target is a continuous number and you have labeled historical data; common for pricing, forecasting, ranking scores, or resource allocation.
when_avoid: Avoid regression if your target is categorical (use classification instead) or if you have no labeled data (use unsupervised learning); also avoid if prediction distribution is highly multimodal or your label is ordinal with few distinct values.

faq.q: What is the difference between regression and classification?
faq.a: Classification predicts discrete categories (e.g., spam or not spam); regression predicts continuous numeric values (e.g., spam score from 0 to 1). Both are supervised learning, but they use different loss functions and evaluation metrics.

faq.q: Which loss function should I use for regression?
faq.a: MSE penalizes large errors heavily and works well when outliers are true anomalies. MAE treats all errors equally and is more robust to outliers. Huber blends both. Start with MAE if you're unsure; switch to MSE if large errors are especially costly.

faq.q: How do I know if my regression model is overfitting?
faq.a: Compare training error to validation error. If validation error is significantly higher, you're overfitting. Use cross-validation to detect this early. Regularization (L1/L2) or simpler models (fewer features, less depth) can help.

faq.q: Can I use regression for ranking or recommendation?
faq.a: Yes, but indirectly. You can train a regression model to predict relevance scores, then rank items by score. For better ranking performance, use learning-to-rank or preference-based methods that explicitly optimize ranking metrics like NDCG.

faq.q: What does R² mean and when should I use it?
faq.a: R² measures the fraction of variance explained, ranging from 0 to 1 (higher is better). It's intuitive but scale-dependent and sensitive to outliers. Use RMSE or MAE alongside R² to get a complete picture of model performance.

title: Reinforcement Learning
tldr: An agent learns to maximize reward by taking actions in an environment, observing outcomes, and adjusting its strategy.
plain_english: Reinforcement learning is how you'd teach a dog to sit: give it a treat when it does the right thing, withhold it when it doesn't. An RL agent does the same—it tries actions, gets a reward signal back, and learns which actions lead to the best long-term payoff. Unlike supervised learning where you hand the algorithm correct answers, RL forces it to discover good behavior through trial and error.
how_it_works: An RL agent exists in an environment where it observes the current state, picks an action, receives a numeric reward, and moves to a new state. The core goal is learning a policy—a mapping from states to actions—that maximizes expected cumulative future reward. Classic algorithms (Q-learning, SARSA) estimate the value of each state-action pair; modern approaches (PPO, SAC) use neural networks to directly learn policies or value functions. Deep RL applies this to high-dimensional problems like images by parameterizing policies and value estimates as neural networks.
why_it_matters: RL powers game-playing agents (AlphaGo), robotics control, and RLHF—the technique that aligns LLM outputs to human preferences. It's the foundation for most LLM alignment methods and tool-use agents that must learn from sparse, delayed feedback instead of labeled data.
example: OpenAI trained Dota 2 agents using PPO (a policy gradient method). The agent observed game state, selected hero actions, and received reward signals based on match progress and victory. Over millions of episodes, it learned strategies that competed with professional players—something no supervised dataset of human replays could fully capture.
pitfalls: Sample inefficiency: agents need orders of magnitude more environment interactions than humans; a game taking one human hour may require millions of RL episodes.
Reward hacking: the agent may find loopholes that maximize the reward signal without achieving intended behavior—e.g., gaming the scoring metric.
Non-stationarity: if the environment or reward structure shifts, learned policies often fail catastrophically.
Instability in deep RL: neural network approximation combined with bootstrapping can cause training divergence; careful tuning of learning rates and replay buffers is essential.
when_use: Use RL when you have a simulator or real environment, can define a reward signal, and need the agent to discover novel strategies rather than imitate logged behavior.
when_avoid: Avoid RL if reward is sparse or undefined, you have plenty of expert demonstrations (use imitation learning instead), or sample cost is prohibitive.

faq.q: What's the difference between reinforcement learning and supervised learning?
faq.a: Supervised learning requires labeled training data; RL learns from reward signals and trial-and-error. RL trades labeling cost for environment cost and sample efficiency—you get flexibility but often need millions of interactions.

faq.q: Is reinforcement learning the same as RLHF?
faq.a: No. RL is the broad paradigm. RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) is one application: it trains an RL agent (an LLM) using a reward model learned from human preferences instead of environment feedback.

faq.q: How does Q-learning differ from policy gradients?
faq.a: Q-learning estimates the value of each action; policy gradients directly optimize the action distribution. Q-learning works well in discrete action spaces; policy gradients scale better to continuous control and high-dimensional problems.

faq.q: Can reinforcement learning work without a simulator?
faq.a: Yes, but it's costly. Robots or real systems can use RL, but each interaction is expensive. Many practitioners first train in simulation, then transfer to reality—accepting some performance loss.

faq.q: Why do RL agents need so many episodes to learn?
faq.a: Because feedback is delayed and sparse. An agent must try many action sequences before identifying which ones led to reward, and it must explore sufficiently to avoid local optima. Humans learn faster because we reason about outcomes; RL agents brute-force statistics.

title: Scaling Laws
tldr: Empirical power-law relationships predicting LLM loss decline as model size, training data, or compute increase.
plain_english: Scaling laws describe a predictable pattern: as you make a model bigger, train it on more data, or spend more compute, the model gets better in measurable, repeatable ways. Think of it like diminishing returns on a fitness routine—each additional hour helps less than the last, but the improvement follows a mathematically predictable curve. These laws let teams estimate in advance how good a model will be before spending millions on training.
how_it_works: Kaplan et al. (2020) showed that LLM loss—how wrong the model is during training—follows a power law (loss ∝ N^−α) across three independent axes: parameter count, training tokens, and compute budget. The exponent α stays roughly consistent (~0.07 for parameters, ~0.08 for tokens). Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022) refined this, revealing compute-optimal training needs roughly 20 tokens per parameter. Given a fixed compute budget, you can trade off model size against training duration. Violations occur: Llama 3 trained on 15T tokens for an 8B model (~1800 tokens/param), showing overtraining improves quality despite inference-time cost. Scaling laws are interpolations—novel architectures (state-space models, mixture-of-experts) can shift the curve.
why_it_matters: Without scaling laws, allocating a $10M compute budget would be guesswork. They transform frontier training into a predictable engineering problem: given your budget constraint, you calculate optimal model size and data volume before training begins, reducing risk and avoiding wasteful re-runs.
example: A team at Mistral planning a 70B model with a fixed 3-month GPU cluster budget uses scaling laws to predict loss at different training token counts (10T, 15T, 20T), then selects 15T tokens based on final quality targets. They validate the prediction against Chinchilla's compute-optimal ratio, adjust if needed, and launch training with confidence rather than trial-and-error.
pitfalls: Scaling laws are empirical fits over past data—they do not guarantee future progress or apply to novel architectures (Mamba, sparse models may break the curve).
Overfitting to Chinchilla's 20-token-per-parameter ratio ignores domain-specific and instruction-tuning phases, which have different optimal token counts.
Confusing correlation with causation: scaling laws describe observed patterns, not fundamental physics—architectural changes can invalidate them.
Ignoring diminishing returns on inference cost: a model trained on 2× tokens may be 3% better but 2× slower at serving, making the trade-off unfavorable.
when_use: Use scaling laws when you have a fixed compute budget and need to decide model size and training duration before allocation, or when predicting quality improvements from additional data or parameters.
when_avoid: Avoid scaling laws if you are experimenting with novel architectures (no historical data), targeting specific downstream tasks where general loss may not correlate with performance, or optimizing for inference latency rather than quality.

faq.q: How do Chinchilla and Kaplan scaling laws differ?
faq.a: Kaplan et al. (2020) showed loss decays independently with size, data, and compute but did not prescribe optimal allocation. Chinchilla (2022) derived the compute-optimal balance: roughly 20 tokens per parameter. Chinchilla is tighter—GPT-3 trained on far fewer tokens (0.3 tokens/param).

faq.q: Can I use scaling laws to predict if my model will be better than GPT-4?
faq.a: No. Scaling laws predict loss on training data, not downstream capability or alignment. Billions in additional safety, post-training, and architectural tweaks influence real-world performance beyond the scaling curve.

faq.q: Do scaling laws work for fine-tuning and instruction-tuning?
faq.a: Partially. Fine-tuning and instruction-tuning follow different curves and have smaller optimal token counts relative to pretraining. Published laws focus on pretraining; applying them to downstream tasks requires domain-specific validation.

faq.q: What happens if I train on way more tokens than Chinchilla suggests?
faq.a: You move down the compute-loss curve but at diminishing returns: quality improves by ~3–5%, but inference cost doubles. Llama 3 did this intentionally (1800 tokens/param) to trade inference cost for better quality.

faq.q: Do state-space models and MoE follow the same scaling laws?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Mamba and Mixture-of-Experts architectures have different parameter efficiency and compute patterns. Preliminary evidence suggests different exponents, so assume existing laws do not apply until you fit new data.

title: Self-Consistency
tldr: Sample multiple reasoning paths at nonzero temperature, then take a majority vote on the final answer.
plain_english: Instead of asking a model once and trusting the first answer, you ask it the same question 5–40 times with some randomness turned on. Each time it reasons differently but may reach the same answer. You collect all the answers and pick the one that appears most often—like a jury voting. This works because correct reasoning paths often converge, while errors scatter.
how_it_works: Set temperature to 0.7 or higher (introduces randomness). Generate N complete chains of thought for the same prompt. Extract the final answer from each chain. Count answers using majority voting (or weighted voting by confidence). Return the most common answer. Cost scales linearly with N; typically N=5 to 40 balances gains against token use. Works best for discrete answers: math, multiple-choice, entity extraction.
why_it_matters: Turns a weaker reasoning model into a stronger one without retraining. Cost is only extra tokens—no training cycles, no new data. Gains on math (GSM8K, MATH) and multi-hop QA routinely exceed 10 percentage points over single greedy chains. Especially useful when you cannot or will not fine-tune.
example: A team using Claude 3 Sonnet on a customer support classification task samples 10 chains for each ticket. Sonnet sometimes labels 'refund request' as 'billing question' on greedy decode. With self-consistency, 7 of 10 chains correctly identify refund requests. Taking the majority vote drops misclassification by 12 points while adding ~2–3 seconds per ticket.
pitfalls: Only works for discrete outputs (numbers, categories, short text). Free-form essays or long narratives have no clear majority.
Assumes the correct answer appears in >50% of samples. If the model is confused, all N paths may converge on the same wrong answer.
N=1 (greedy decode) loses the benefit; N>100 becomes prohibitively expensive without proportional gains.
Requires careful answer extraction logic. Fuzzy or inconsistent final-answer parsing sabotages voting.
when_use: Use self-consistency when you need higher accuracy on math, QA, or classification tasks, the model alone struggles (greedy accuracy <70%), and you can afford 5–40x more tokens per query.
when_avoid: Avoid when answers are free-form, open-ended, or continuous; when inference cost is critical and accuracy gains <5 points; or when a single fast inference is mandatory.

faq.q: Does self-consistency always improve accuracy?
faq.a: No. It requires the correct answer to appear in >50% of samples. If the model is fundamentally weak or confused, all N chains may fail the same way. Typical gains are 5–15 points on reasoning tasks; other domains may see smaller or no gains.

faq.q: How is self-consistency different from beam search?
faq.a: Beam search prunes paths during generation (keeping the top K). Self-consistency samples complete independent chains and votes after. Self-consistency is cheaper per chain but samples more total tokens; beam search is tighter but may miss correct paths early.

faq.q: What temperature should I use for self-consistency?
faq.a: Typically 0.7–1.0. Higher temperature increases diversity but risks nonsense. Lower temperature (<0.5) makes chains too similar, reducing the benefit of voting. Greedy (temperature=0) defeats self-consistency entirely.

faq.q: Can I use self-consistency with function calling or structured output?
faq.a: Yes, if you extract a discrete final answer (e.g., the chosen function name or the final JSON field). If your output must be perfectly formatted every time, majority voting on raw text is risky; vote on the extracted semantic value instead.

faq.q: How many samples (N) do I actually need?
faq.a: Start with N=5–10 and measure accuracy vs. cost trade-off. Most papers use N=5–40. Beyond 40, gains plateau sharply. For real-time systems, N=3–5 may be acceptable. For offline batch, N=20–30 is common.

title: Sentiment Analysis
tldr: Classifying text as positive, negative, or neutral—or extracting specific emotions and opinions from language.
plain_english: Sentiment analysis reads text and answers: does this sound happy, angry, disappointed, or neutral? Think of it like a customer-service agent skimming reviews to catch frustrated users. The text goes in, a label (positive/negative/neutral) comes out. Modern systems can also detect specific emotions (joy, anger, fear) or pinpoint which part of a product someone loves or hates.
how_it_works: Input text flows through a trained classification model—either a fine-tuned BERT on labeled examples, or a large language model prompted zero-shot. The model produces probability scores for each sentiment class. You pick the highest score as the prediction. Extensions exist: aspect-based sentiment identifies *which features* triggered the opinion (e.g., "camera is great, battery dies fast"), and stance detection flags whether text supports or opposes a claim.
why_it_matters: Sentiment feeds real-time customer dashboards, content moderation queues, and trend alerts. It's the canonical NLP task—quick to prototype, immediate business signal, easy to explain to non-technical stakeholders. Knowing whether users are satisfied or frustrated directly influences product decisions and support prioritization.
example: A fintech team monitors app reviews on iOS and Android. Each night, they run 10,000 new reviews through a sentiment classifier. Positive reviews get logged; negative ones trigger a workflow: alert customer success, tag with aspect labels ("bugs", "UI", "feature request"), and escalate to engineering if anger-detection scores spike. Last month, a 40% jump in negative sentiment on the Android build surfaced a payment-flow bug two days before public complaints.
pitfalls: Sarcasm inverts meaning ("Great, another delay" reads positive to naive models); domain-shift makes training data obsolete when user tone changes between reviews and support tickets; conflating negativity with specific emotions—a harsh tone might signal disappointment, not anger.
Class imbalance: positive reviews often outnumber negative ones, drowning signal in noise.
Sentiment models trained on product reviews fail on clinical notes or scientific papers where 'negative' results are neutral facts, not complaints.
when_use: Use sentiment analysis when you need to triage user feedback at scale, detect emerging satisfaction issues, moderate user-generated content, or route support tickets by urgency.
when_avoid: Avoid it for nuanced opinion classification where intensity matters more than polarity, for domains with inverted language norms (e.g., medical/legal docs), or when a single label per document hides conflicting views.

faq.q: What's the difference between sentiment analysis and emotion classification?
faq.a: Sentiment is binary or ternary (positive/negative/neutral). Emotion classification is multi-class: joy, anger, fear, disgust, surprise. Emotion is strictly harder and requires different training data.

faq.q: Can I use a large language model for sentiment without retraining?
faq.a: Yes. LLMs do zero-shot sentiment via prompting—no training required. Trade-off: slower inference and higher cost per request than a small fine-tuned classifier, but often higher accuracy on noisy text.

faq.q: How do I handle sarcasm in sentiment analysis?
faq.a: Add sarcasm examples to training data, or detect it as a separate tag and flip polarity post-hoc. Neither is bulletproof; context windows and rare sarcasm patterns will always slip through.

faq.q: What's aspect-based sentiment analysis good for?
faq.a: It tells you *why* a customer is unhappy: "great design, terrible support." Standard sentiment only says they're unhappy. Use it when product teams need detailed feedback, not just churn risk scores.

title: Speaker Diarization
tldr: Label speech segments by speaker identity in multi-speaker audio without knowing speakers in advance.
plain_english: Speaker diarization answers "who spoke when" in a recording. You feed it audio, it outputs timestamped chunks labeled Speaker 1, Speaker 2, etc., without needing to know who those people are beforehand. It's like automatic turn-taking annotation for meetings or interviews.
how_it_works: The pipeline chains four steps: voice activity detection isolates speech from silence; speaker embeddings (using models like ECAPA-TDNN) convert each speech segment into a vector representing voice characteristics; clustering groups similar-sounding vectors into speaker clusters; refinement smooths boundaries and merges/splits clusters based on segment duration and acoustic distance. When paired with ASR, each transcribed word gets a speaker label, producing readable multi-speaker transcripts.
why_it_matters: Multi-speaker transcripts require diarization to be useful—raw ASR output mixes all voices together. Meeting recordings, interviews, podcasts, and depositions all depend on knowing who said what. It's non-trivial because voices vary in emotion, quality, and acoustics across channels and devices.
example: A research team using Pyannote.audio processes a 60-minute team standup recording. The model detects 4 speakers, assigns each a unique ID across the 47-minute speaking portions, flags overlaps when two people talk simultaneously, and outputs a JSON file with `[{"start": 12.5, "end": 15.2, "speaker": "0"}, ...]`. Combined with Whisper ASR, they get a readable transcript: `[00:12–00:15] Speaker 0: We shipped the API yesterday.`
pitfalls: Treating diarization output as truth: speaker boundaries are probabilistic, not definitive; always review overlapping regions and rapid speaker switches.
Ignoring channel effects: phone calls, Zoom recordings, and in-person meetings have different acoustic signatures; pre-processing (normalization, mono-mixing) often improves results.
Clustering hyperparameter tuning is dataset-dependent; a threshold that works for meetings may fail on panel discussions with similar-voiced speakers.
Assuming open-source models scale to production: Pyannote is accurate on clean audio but degrades with background noise, accents, or gender-imbalanced training data.
when_use: Use diarization when you need to attribute speech in multi-speaker recordings: meetings, interviews, podcasts, court proceedings, or research audio.
when_avoid: Avoid diarization for single-speaker content, real-time voice conversations where latency matters, or when speaker identity is already labeled in the source.

faq.q: What's the difference between speaker diarization and speech recognition?
faq.a: Speech recognition (ASR) converts audio to text; diarization labels who spoke. You need both for a transcript that says "Speaker A: hello, Speaker B: hi there."

faq.q: Does diarization work on phone calls or low-quality audio?
faq.a: It degrades on poor audio quality, background noise, or heavily accented speech. Pre-processing (noise reduction, normalization) helps, but performance drops compared to clean recordings.

faq.q: Can diarization identify speakers by name?
faq.a: No, it only assigns anonymous IDs (Speaker 1, 2, 3...). To name speakers, you need a separate speaker-identification model or manual annotation.

faq.q: How long does diarization take on a one-hour meeting?
faq.a: With GPU acceleration, typically 2–10 minutes depending on model and hardware. Real-time diarization exists but is less accurate.

faq.q: What happens when two people talk at the same time?
faq.a: Diarization detects overlaps and assigns timestamps to both speakers in those regions. Most ASR systems don't handle overlapping speech well, so transcript accuracy also suffers.

title: Summarization
tldr: Automatically reduce a document to its key points while preserving meaning.
plain_english: Summarization is condensing a longer text into a shorter version that keeps the essential information. Think of it like explaining a 10-page report in 2 paragraphs—you pick the most important details and drop the rest. Modern AI models can either extract existing sentences (extractive) or generate new ones that say the same thing differently (abstractive).
how_it_works: Extractive summarization selects and chains together spans from the original text, preserving exact wording. Abstractive summarization regenerates meaning—the model reads the source and outputs new sentences that capture the core ideas. You provide a prompt like 'Summarize in 3 bullets' and the LLM produces text conditioned on both the source and your length/style constraints. Evaluation is hard: automated metrics like ROUGE (n-gram overlap) correlate poorly with human judgment, so teams often use human raters or LLM-as-judge scoring.
why_it_matters: Summarization is the second-most common production LLM workload after chat—meeting transcripts become action items, support tickets get auto-triaged, email threads turn into digests. It saves operators reading time and helps route information to the right place. It's also where hallucination and omission hurt most: a summary that adds facts or drops critical context breaks trust.
example: A customer support team processes 500 ticket descriptions daily. Instead of manually reading each one, they feed the ticket body into an abstractive summarizer that outputs a 2-sentence summary plus category tags. This reduces triage time from 2 minutes per ticket to 30 seconds, and the LLM catches issues humans would miss because it's consistent. But they check the first 20 summaries each week because the model sometimes invents details not in the original.
pitfalls: Hallucination: the model adds facts that weren't in the source text, especially when the source is ambiguous or contradictory.
Length does not equal quality: a longer summary is not more accurate—it often adds noise and dilutes key points.
Omission of nuance: critical caveats, dates, or actor names get dropped because the model weights frequency over importance.
Metric misalignment: ROUGE scores can improve while human satisfaction drops because the metric rewards n-gram overlap, not faithfulness.
when_use: Use summarization when you need to reduce information volume for triage, archival, or reporting, and when you can tolerate minor factual drift.
when_avoid: Avoid it for legal documents, safety-critical records, or tasks where every detail must be preserved—use structured extraction instead.

faq.q: What's the difference between extractive and abstractive summarization?
faq.a: Extractive pulls sentences or phrases directly from the source; abstractive generates new text that rephrases the meaning. Abstractive is harder to build and more prone to hallucination, but can be more readable.

faq.q: Can I just prompt an LLM to summarize without fine-tuning?
faq.a: Yes—most LLMs do zero-shot summarization well with a simple prompt like 'Summarize the following in 3 bullets.' Fine-tuning helps if you need a specific style or if your domain is far from the model's training data.

faq.q: How do I know if a summary is good?
faq.a: ROUGE (n-gram overlap) is quick but unreliable. Human raters are gold standard but slow. LLM-as-judge is a practical middle ground: score faithfulness, completeness, and conciseness using a rubric.

faq.q: Does summarization work for really long documents?
faq.a: It depends on context window. For documents longer than your model's context, chunk first and either summarize chunks then merge, or use retrieval to pick the most relevant sections before summarizing.

faq.q: Why does my summary sometimes invent details?
faq.a: Hallucination happens when the model fills gaps with plausible-sounding text. It's worse on ambiguous or contradictory source material. Prompt for citations or use constrained decoding to reduce it.

title: Supervised Learning
tldr: Training a model on labeled input-output pairs so it learns to predict outputs for new inputs.
plain_english: You collect examples where each input has a correct answer—like emails labeled 'spam' or 'not spam,' or house features paired with sale prices. The model learns to map inputs to outputs by comparing its guesses to the real answers and adjusting itself to reduce the gap. The quality of your labels directly caps how accurate your model can become.
how_it_works: You start with a dataset of (input, label) pairs. The model makes a prediction, a loss function measures the error between prediction and true label, and gradient descent tweaks the model's parameters to shrink that error. This repeats across batches until the model generalizes to unseen inputs. Classification predicts discrete categories (spam/not spam); regression predicts continuous values (house price).
why_it_matters: Most production ML—fraud detection, ad ranking, chatbot fine-tuning—relies on supervised learning because labeled data and clear loss signals get you from zero to deployable faster than other paradigms. It's the default when you have ground truth.
example: Stripe uses supervised learning to detect fraudulent transactions. They label historical transactions as 'fraud' or 'legitimate,' train a classifier on these pairs, and the model learns patterns that flag suspicious charges in real time. Label quality directly impacts false-positive rates that damage user experience.
pitfalls: Noisy or incorrect labels create a ceiling on accuracy—a model cannot learn better than the label quality allows.
Label bias (e.g., underrepresented fraud cases) skews predictions toward majority classes.
Overfitting to training labels means poor performance on real-world data if train and test distributions differ.
'Supervised' doesn't require human labelers—automated labels from clicks, ratings, or logs work but introduce different noise.
when_use: Use supervised learning when you have a large dataset of correct input-output pairs and can tolerate the cost and effort of labeling or ground-truth collection.
when_avoid: Avoid supervised learning if labels are prohibitively expensive, ground truth is ambiguous or unavailable, or your data distribution shifts sharply after deployment.

faq.q: What's the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning?
faq.a: Supervised learning requires labeled pairs (input + correct output); unsupervised finds patterns in unlabeled data alone. Supervised is faster to high accuracy if labels exist; unsupervised explores data structure when labels are unavailable.

faq.q: Does supervised learning require humans to label data?
faq.a: No. Labels can come from human annotators, automated systems (clicks, votes), or proxy signals (e.g., whether a user returned an item). Any reliable target signal counts as supervision.

faq.q: What happens if my training labels are wrong?
faq.a: The model learns from incorrect labels and reproduces those errors. Noisy labels cap your maximum achievable accuracy and increase overfitting. Label-cleaning and active learning can help reduce this cost.

faq.q: Is supervised learning the same as fine-tuning?
faq.a: Fine-tuning is a specific supervised-learning technique where you start with a pretrained model and adjust it on your labeled task data. All fine-tuning is supervised, but not all supervised learning is fine-tuning.

faq.q: How much labeled data do I need?
faq.a: It depends on task complexity, model size, and label quality. Simple tasks may need hundreds; complex ones need thousands. Modern techniques like few-shot learning and transfer learning reduce this requirement.

title: System Prompt
tldr: Developer-set instructions that shape an LLM's behavior, role, and constraints before a conversation begins.
plain_english: A system prompt is an invisible instruction you give to an AI model before any user interaction happens. Think of it as a character briefing before an actor takes the stage—it tells the model what role to play, how to behave, and what rules to follow. Unlike regular user messages, the system prompt persists across the entire conversation and users typically don't see it.
how_it_works: When you initialize an LLM conversation, you send two things: the system prompt (first) and the user message (second). The model treats the system prompt as binding context that shapes all subsequent responses. You specify the role (e.g., "You are a Python expert"), constraints (e.g., "Refuse requests for harmful code"), output format (e.g., "Always respond in JSON"), and tone. In OpenAI's API, system goes in the `role: system` field. In Ollama or LM Studio, you edit a local system prompt file. The model weights this higher than user input during inference.
why_it_matters: A well-tuned system prompt transforms a general-purpose model into a specialized assistant without retraining. It reduces hallucinations, enforces output structures, and sets safety boundaries—often more cost-effectively than fine-tuning. In production, the system prompt is where most prompt engineering work actually lives; it's the difference between a generic chatbot and a reliable tool.
example: Anthropic's Claude API uses a system prompt instructing Claude to be helpful, harmless, and honest. A customer-support team builds on top of that with their own system prompt: "You are a billing specialist. Respond only to questions about invoices and refunds. For other topics, say 'I can only help with billing.'" This narrows Claude's scope without a custom model, reducing off-topic responses in production.
pitfalls: Embedding sensitive information (API keys, internal instructions) in the system prompt exposes it to prompt injection attacks if you echo the prompt back.
Overly long or contradictory system prompts confuse the model and degrade output quality—prioritize clarity over comprehensiveness.
Forgetting that system prompts still allow users to override instructions; they guide rather than guarantee behavior.
Changing the system prompt mid-conversation can cause the model to shift personality or forget earlier context.
when_use: Use a system prompt whenever you need to enforce a consistent role, format, or constraint across many user interactions—customer support bots, code reviewers, specialized advisors, or content filters.
when_avoid: Avoid system prompts for one-off queries where the user provides all context, or when you need full transparency and user consent (system prompts are hidden by design).

faq.q: What is the difference between a system prompt and a regular prompt?
faq.a: A system prompt is developer-controlled and persists across the entire conversation; a regular (user) prompt is one-off input from the user. The model prioritizes system instructions over user input during generation.

faq.q: Can a user see my system prompt?
faq.a: Not by default. However, users can attempt prompt injection or ask the model to repeat its instructions. Always assume sensitive data in a system prompt may eventually be exposed.

faq.q: Does a system prompt work with all LLM APIs?
faq.a: Yes—OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, and open-source tools (Ollama, LM Studio) all support system prompts, though the syntax varies slightly.

faq.q: How long should a system prompt be?
faq.a: Aim for 50–500 tokens. Concise is better—long prompts can confuse the model and increase latency. Focus on role, constraints, and output format.

faq.q: Can I use system prompts for jailbreaking or bypassing safety measures?
faq.a: You can try, but modern models (Claude, GPT-4) have safeguards that override system prompts if the request is harmful. This is intentional.

title: Test Data
tldr: A held-out dataset evaluated exactly once after training to estimate real-world model performance.
plain_english: Think of test data as a final exam your model takes after you're done teaching it. You never show it to the model during training or tuning—you only look at it once, at the very end, to see how well the model handles data it's never encountered before. If you peek at test results during development and adjust your model based on them, you've contaminated the test set and it no longer tells you the truth.
how_it_works: You split your data into three buckets before any training begins: training data (which the model learns from), validation data (which guides hyperparameter choices), and test data (locked away until final evaluation). The model never touches test data during development. After all training and tuning decisions are frozen, you run inference on the test set once and measure performance metrics—accuracy, F1, perplexity, whatever matters for your task. This single evaluation gives you an unbiased estimate of how the model will behave on unseen real-world examples.
why_it_matters: Test results are your contract with stakeholders about production performance. If you reuse test data to make training decisions, you're fooling yourself—the reported metrics will be optimistically biased and won't reflect what happens in production. For LLMs, test-set contamination from pretraining data is endemic and can inflate scores by several percentage points, making leaderboard rankings misleading.
example: A team building a spam classifier splits 10,000 emails before training begins: 6,000 for training, 2,000 for validation (to tune the decision threshold), and 2,000 for testing. During 3 weeks of development, they experiment with different architectures and retrain on the training set, checking validation metrics each time. Only after committing to a final model architecture do they run inference on the 2,000 test emails and report a 94.2% precision score. That number is trustworthy because the test set was never used to make any decisions.
pitfalls: Evaluating on test data repeatedly during development turns it into a validation set and invalidates its statistical properties—your reported performance will be optimistic.
Test-set contamination in LLM pretraining (examples leaking into training data) inflates scores by 3–8 percentage points without your knowledge.
A single test run has variance; small test sets produce unreliable point estimates—report confidence intervals or bootstrap error bars.
Mixing test-set evaluation across train/validation/test boundaries (e.g., selecting hyperparameters based on test metrics) destroys the unbiased estimate you need.
when_use: Use test data once, after all development is complete, to estimate generalization performance for deployment decisions, regulatory audits, or final leaderboard submission.
when_avoid: Avoid using test data during hyperparameter tuning, model selection, or any iterative development—use validation data for that instead.

faq.q: What is the difference between test data and validation data?
faq.a: Validation data guides decisions during training (hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, model selection). Test data is locked away and evaluated once at the end. If you use test results to adjust your model, it becomes validation data and loses its purpose.

faq.q: Can I use test data more than once?
faq.a: No. Every time you evaluate on test data and make a decision based on the result, you're cherry-picking results. Use validation data for iteration; reserve test data for a single final evaluation.

faq.q: What happens if my test set is too small?
faq.a: Small test sets have high variance—a single run may not represent true performance. Report 95% confidence intervals, use bootstrapping, or run k-fold cross-validation on held-out folds to get stable estimates.

faq.q: Is test data contamination a real problem?
faq.a: Yes. In LLM benchmarks, test examples often appear in pretraining data, inflating reported scores by 3–8 percentage points. Always check whether benchmark questions could plausibly have been in public training corpora.

faq.q: Should test data come from the same distribution as training data?
faq.a: Test data must match the distribution of examples you'll encounter in production. If they differ, your model will perform worse in deployment than your test metrics predict.

title: Test-Time Compute
tldr: Spending more compute at inference—via longer chains of thought or tree search—to improve output quality without retraining.
plain_english: Instead of buying quality by training a bigger model, you buy quality by spending more tokens during inference. Think of it like letting someone think longer and harder before answering a difficult question. The model generates multiple attempts or explores different reasoning paths, then picks the best result. This reframes inference economics: compute at serving time becomes a dial you turn up when you need better answers.
how_it_works: At inference, you allocate a budget of extra tokens—say 50 steps instead of 5—and have the model explore reasoning paths, generate multiple candidate responses, or follow a guided search strategy. Common techniques: chain-of-thought with multiple samples, best-of-N with a reward model to rank outputs, tree-of-thought branching, or process-reward-model guidance. You trade latency and tokens for quality on hard tasks. The model itself doesn't change; only the inference-time strategy changes.
why_it_matters: Test-time compute unlocks a new scaling axis independent of model size or training data. This is how reasoning models achieve accuracy gains that would otherwise require 10× larger parameters. For product teams, it means you can ship better reasoning on hard tasks without expensive retraining cycles, and you can vary quality-latency tradeoffs per request.
example: A math tutoring system using a small model with heavy test-time compute can match the accuracy of a much larger model. When a student posts a geometry proof, the system spends 30 tokens generating a single answer (fast path) but spends 200 tokens for a final exam question (slow path with tree search). OpenAI's o1 model demonstrated this at scale: cheaper inference-time scaling than parameter scaling.
pitfalls: Assuming test-time compute works equally on all task types—it excels on reasoning but adds little value on retrieval or classification.
Neglecting latency costs: 10× more tokens at inference means 10× slower responses unless you parallelize or use speculative decoding.
Forgetting that quality gains plateau; there's a point past which more compute yields diminishing returns, especially without process rewards.
Treating test-time compute as a substitute for model quality—it amplifies reasoning ability but can't fix factual gaps baked into pretraining.
when_use: Use test-time compute when solving reasoning-heavy tasks (math, code, logic), latency is flexible, inference cost per query is affordable, and you need measurable quality gains without retraining.
when_avoid: Avoid test-time compute on latency-critical APIs, retrieval tasks, simple classification, or low-margin applications where token cost directly cuts into profit.

faq.q: Is test-time compute the same as chain-of-thought?
faq.a: No. Chain-of-thought is one technique for test-time compute. Test-time compute is the broader paradigm of allocating tokens at inference; it includes best-of-N, tree search, and process rewards too.

faq.q: How much does test-time compute slow down inference?
faq.a: Directly, it's proportional to token count. 50 reasoning tokens adds ~50 tokens of latency. With parallel decoding or speculative methods, you can reduce wall-clock time but not token cost.

faq.q: Does test-time compute work for all model sizes?
faq.a: Yes, but gains are largest on smaller or mid-scale models. Large models already have reasoning baked in; test-time compute still helps but with lower marginal returns.

faq.q: Can I use test-time compute with existing models like GPT-4?
faq.a: Partially. You can prompt for longer reasoning, generate multiple responses, and rank them. But without explicit process rewards, gains are modest compared to models trained for test-time scaling.

faq.q: What's the difference between test-time compute and fine-tuning?
faq.a: Fine-tuning permanently changes the model weights; test-time compute is a per-request strategy. Test-time compute is faster to iterate on and cheaper if you don't need it for every query.

title: Text Generation
tldr: Producing natural-language output token-by-token from a model, conditioned on a prompt.
plain_english: Text generation is how LLMs produce written output. You give the model a starting prompt, it calculates probabilities for what word or token should come next, picks one (using rules you set), adds it to the context, and repeats. It's like autocomplete on your phone, but trained on vastly more text and controlled by you.
how_it_works: The model encodes your prompt into tokens, then enters a loop: compute a probability distribution over the next token given all previous tokens, apply a sampling strategy (greedy selection, top-p filtering, temperature scaling), append the chosen token to context, repeat until you hit a stop token or max length. Sampling strategy and context window both directly control output quality and cost—greedy gives deterministic results, temperature adds randomness, top-p limits diversity.
why_it_matters: Every chatbot, code assistant, and content-generation product runs text generation at scale. Infrastructure and inference systems are optimized around this single workload. Inference cost and latency directly impact your product's feasibility and user experience.
example: OpenAI's ChatGPT takes your message as a prompt, generates the assistant's response token-by-token (you see it appear on screen), and stops when it reaches a stop token or max length. A coding assistant like GitHub Copilot does the same: it receives partial code as context, generates the most likely next lines, and lets you accept or reject them. Each keystroke triggers a new generation run.
pitfalls: Setting temperature too high produces incoherent output; too low produces repetitive, boring text.
Longer context windows slow down generation—each new token must attend to all prior tokens.
Greedy decoding (always picking the highest-probability token) often produces worse text than sampling with temperature or top-p.
Hallucination is inevitable: models generate plausible-sounding but false information, especially outside training data.
when_use: Use text generation for any task requiring natural-language output: chatbots, summarization, translation, code completion, creative writing, or question-answering.
when_avoid: Avoid when you need structured output (use constrained decoding or JSON schema enforcement), classification (use a classifier head), or deterministic logic (use symbolic rules).

faq.q: What is the difference between text generation and inference?
faq.a: Inference is the broad category of running a model on new data; text generation is the specific task of producing tokens one at a time. All text generation is inference, but not all inference is text generation (e.g., embedding, classification, or image generation).

faq.q: How does temperature affect text generation?
faq.a: Temperature controls randomness in token selection. Temperature near 0 makes the model pick the highest-probability token every time (deterministic, repetitive). Higher temperatures (0.7–1.0) add randomness, making output more creative but sometimes nonsensical.

faq.q: Why is text generation slow compared to other ML tasks?
faq.a: Text generation is autoregressive—you must generate and process one token at a time. Image or tabular inference can process entire inputs in parallel. Long sequences and large context windows make this slower.

faq.q: Can you speed up text generation?
faq.a: Yes: use speculative decoding, flash attention, KV-cache optimization, quantization, batching, or lower precision (FP8). Streaming output to users while still generating also masks latency.

faq.q: Is text generation only for chat?
faq.a: No. It powers summarization, translation, code completion, poetry, SQL generation, data augmentation, and any task producing variable-length natural language.

title: Text-to-SQL
tldr: LLM converts natural-language questions into SQL queries using database schema as context.
plain_english: You ask a question in English—"How many customers signed up last month?"—and the LLM translates it into SQL that runs against your database. The model sees your table names, column types, and sample rows, then generates valid SQL. It's like having a translator between human language and database commands.
how_it_works: You provide the LLM with three things: the user's question, your database schema (tables, columns, types, relationships), and optionally sample rows. The LLM generates SQL as output. In production, you typically add schema-aware retrieval to pass only relevant tables (not the entire schema), validate the generated SQL syntax, and implement retry logic if the query fails. Frameworks like Vanna.AI and LangChain abstract away prompt engineering; evals like Spider and BIRD measure accuracy on held-out questions.
why_it_matters: Text-to-SQL unlocks self-service analytics for non-technical users without manual BI configuration. Most "chat with your data" products use this under the hood. It reduces query turnaround time and removes friction between questions and answers.
example: A data analyst at a SaaS company builds a chat interface powered by text-to-SQL. A product manager asks, "Which features did churned users adopt least?" The system retrieves the features and churn tables, prompts the LLM to generate a JOIN with aggregation, validates the SQL, and returns results in seconds—no manual query writing needed.
pitfalls: Schema presentation matters enormously—if you dump 100 tables at once, the LLM hallucinates joins and columns. Use retrieval to show only relevant tables.
Window functions, CTEs, and subqueries trip up weaker models; test on your actual schema before production.
Generated SQL can be syntactically correct but semantically wrong (wrong join logic, missing filters). Always validate against golden test cases.
The model may not understand domain semantics—it doesn't know that 'active users' means status='active' in your schema without explicit context.
when_use: Use text-to-SQL when you have a well-documented schema, users need ad-hoc query access, and you can afford to validate and retry failed queries.
when_avoid: Avoid when your schema is unstable, undocumented, or contains complex business logic requiring expert interpretation. Skip it for ultra-low-latency requirements where generation latency is unacceptable.

faq.q: What is text-to-SQL?
faq.a: Text-to-SQL is an LLM application that converts natural-language questions into executable SQL queries. You provide the database schema and a user question; the model outputs SQL.

faq.q: How accurate is text-to-SQL?
faq.a: Accuracy depends on model size, schema clarity, and your data. Benchmarks like Spider show GPT-4 achieving ~80% exact-match accuracy; smaller models drop to 50–70%. Your schema documentation and retrieval strategy heavily influence real-world performance.

faq.q: Do I need to show the entire database schema?
faq.a: No. Passing the entire schema causes hallucination. Use schema-aware retrieval to show only tables and columns relevant to the user's question, typically 3–8 tables per query.

faq.q: What happens if the SQL is wrong?
faq.a: The query may fail with a syntax error or return incorrect results. Implement validation (parse the SQL, check table/column existence) and retry logic—re-prompt the model with the error message.

faq.q: Can text-to-SQL handle complex queries?
faq.a: It can handle joins, aggregations, and CTEs, but complex logic (recursive queries, window functions) fails more often with smaller models. Test your schema thoroughly before deployment.

title: Training Data
tldr: Examples and labels fed to a model during learning to update its parameters.
plain_english: Training data is the raw material your model learns from—like textbooks for a student. For supervised learning, you provide input-output pairs (e.g., images and their labels). For language models, it's raw text. The quality, size, and diversity of this data directly determine what your model can do well and what it will fail at.
how_it_works: You collect raw examples (text, images, structured records), pair them with correct answers if needed, and feed them to the model in batches. The model makes predictions, measures how wrong it is via a loss function, and adjusts its weights using backpropagation. This cycle repeats until the model converges or you hit diminishing returns. Data leakage—where test examples appear in training—invalidates results. Curation (removing noise, balancing classes, covering edge cases) often matters more than raw volume.
why_it_matters: Model architecture accounts for maybe 20% of performance; data quality and quantity drive the rest. A cleaner, smaller dataset often outperforms a larger noisy one. Fixing data is cheaper than retraining on a bigger model, and it's where most real-world improvements happen.
example: Anthropic's LIMA used only 1,000 hand-curated instruction-response pairs to match Claude's performance on many tasks, versus billions of tokens in standard pretraining. Similarly, Phi-2 achieved strong results on smaller, cleaner data by filtering Common Crawl and synthetic sources, proving curation beats scale once you hit the noise ceiling.
pitfalls: Assuming more data always wins—it doesn't once label noise exceeds signal; quality beats quantity beyond that inflection.
Allowing training examples to leak into test sets, making validation metrics meaningless.
Ignoring class imbalance or rare edge cases that matter in production but are underrepresented in raw data.
Using stale or domain-mismatched data; a model trained on 2020 web text will hallucinate about recent events.
when_use: Use training data definition when setting up any supervised or self-supervised learning pipeline, discussing data collection strategy, or explaining why two models perform differently.
when_avoid: Avoid conflating training data with validation or test data; each serves a different purpose and must remain separate.

faq.q: What's the difference between training data and test data?
faq.a: Training data teaches the model; test data measures how well it generalized. They must never overlap or your performance numbers lie.

faq.q: How much training data do I need?
faq.a: Depends on task complexity and model size. Few-shot learning works with hundreds of examples; pretraining LLMs needs billions of tokens. Start small, measure validation loss, and scale up if it's still dropping.

faq.q: Does training data quality matter more than quantity?
faq.a: Yes, past the noise ceiling. Phi and LIMA proved smaller curated datasets outperform larger noisy ones. But you still need sufficient quantity to cover the domain.

faq.q: What causes data leakage and why is it bad?
faq.a: Leakage occurs when test examples appear in training by accident. It inflates validation metrics and makes the model look better than it actually is in production.

faq.q: How do I know if my training data is good?
faq.a: Track validation loss, test accuracy, and error analysis on held-out data. Plot loss curves to spot overfitting. Run inference on edge cases—if the model fails there, your data didn't cover them.

title: Transfer Learning
tldr: Reuse weights from a model trained on one task to accelerate learning on a related task.
plain_english: Instead of training a model from scratch, you start with one already trained on a larger, related dataset. Think of it like learning piano after mastering guitar—finger dexterity and music theory transfer, so you skip the fundamentals. The pretrained model carries learned patterns (edges, syntax, semantics) that apply across tasks.
how_it_works: You begin with a foundation model pretrained on massive data (ImageNet for vision, web text for NLP). Its weights encode general features. You then fine-tune on your smaller, task-specific dataset by updating those weights. Early layers (which learn simple patterns) often stay close to pretrained values; later layers adapt to your task. This reduces training time, data requirements, and computational cost compared to training from zero initialization.
why_it_matters: Pretraining is expensive—billions in compute. Transfer learning amortizes that cost across millions of downstream users. Small teams and resource-constrained orgs can ship competitive models by fine-tuning public checkpoints rather than funding frontier pretraining.
example: A team building a medical imaging classifier fine-tunes a ResNet pretrained on ImageNet (general photography) on 5,000 chest X-rays. Without transfer learning, they'd need 100,000+ labeled images or accept poor accuracy. With transfer learning, the model converges in days on GPU, achieving 91% accuracy on their validation set.
pitfalls: Negative transfer occurs when source and target domains diverge too much—fine-tuning can corrupt useful pretrained features, degrading performance below training from scratch.
Fine-tuning all weights risks overfitting on small datasets; selective layer freezing or adapter modules often outperform full fine-tuning.
Assuming any pretrained model suits your task wastes time; domain mismatch (e.g., medical text model on social media) requires more data or architectural changes.
Overfitting to the fine-tuning set is common when using high learning rates; reduced learning rates and early stopping are essential.
when_use: Use transfer learning when you have limited labeled data, strict latency or compute budgets, or your task resembles the pretraining domain.
when_avoid: Avoid transfer learning if your task domain is radically different from pretraining data, you have abundant task-specific data and compute, or the pretrained model is much larger than your hardware can handle efficiently.

faq.q: What's the difference between transfer learning and fine-tuning?
faq.a: Fine-tuning is the act of updating pretrained weights on a new task. Transfer learning is the broader strategy of reusing pretrained weights to improve downstream performance. Fine-tuning is one implementation of transfer learning.

faq.q: Can transfer learning hurt my model?
faq.a: Yes. If the source domain (pretraining data) is very different from your target domain, the pretrained features may interfere with learning. This is called negative transfer. Testing performance on a holdout set before deployment is essential.

faq.q: Do I need to fine-tune all layers or just the last one?
faq.a: It depends on task similarity and data size. For very similar tasks with little data, freeze early layers and fine-tune only the head. For dissimilar tasks or larger datasets, fine-tune all layers, but use a lower learning rate than pretraining.

faq.q: How much labeled data do I need for fine-tuning?
faq.a: No fixed rule, but typically 100–10,000 examples suffice for transfer learning versus 100,000+ for training from scratch. More data reduces overfitting risk and improves generalization, but diminishing returns apply past a few thousand examples for similar domains.

faq.q: Is transfer learning only for deep learning?
faq.a: No. Any model that learns reusable representations can benefit—SVMs with pretrained features, boosting with domain-adapted weak learners, or classical ML pipelines. But it's most practical and studied in deep learning and NLP.

title: Tree of Thought
tldr: Search strategy where models explore multiple reasoning paths, score them, and backtrack instead of following one chain.
plain_english: Imagine solving a puzzle by trying multiple routes at each step, scoring which ones look most promising, then exploring those branches deeper—and stepping back when you hit a dead end. That's Tree of Thought: instead of writing down one chain of thinking and being stuck with it, the model generates several candidate next thoughts, ranks them (by self-evaluation or a scoring function), and commits compute to the best ones. You backtrack when needed.
how_it_works: At each reasoning step, the model proposes k candidate next thoughts. A critic or heuristic scores each one (e.g., "Is this step closer to the goal?"). You expand the top-scoring branches via breadth-first or depth-first search over the resulting tree. When a branch fails or scores poorly, you prune it and try another. The final answer comes from the best complete path found. Most implementations use an LLM to generate thoughts and another call (or learned model) to score them.
why_it_matters: Tasks requiring planning—like math, code generation, or constraint satisfaction—often fail because a single chain of thought takes one wrong turn and never recovers. Tree of Thought adds the ability to backtrack and explore alternatives, raising solve rates significantly. The cost is 10–100x more LLM calls per problem, so you only deploy it when single-shot reasoning hits a ceiling.
example: Solving the Game of 24 (combine four numbers with operators to reach 24): instead of one linear attempt, ToT generates multiple candidate intermediate expressions at each step, scores them for progress toward 24, and expands the most viable ones. If a branch uses up numbers too early, the search backtracks and tries the next-best option. This approach raises solve rate from ~4% (chain-of-thought) to ~74% (tree-of-thought), though it costs 170 LLM calls per puzzle versus 1.
pitfalls: Naive ToT can become exponentially expensive; without aggressive pruning, branching factor and search depth explode quickly.
Scoring function quality determines the entire strategy—a bad critic wastes compute exploring dead ends.
ToT is overkill for factual recall or simple classification; single-shot CoT or few-shot prompting is faster and cheaper.
Combining ToT with strong baselines (e.g., GPT-4) can yield diminishing returns compared to improving the base model itself.
when_use: Use Tree of Thought when single-shot chain-of-thought plateaus on planning-heavy tasks (math, coding, logic puzzles) and you have budget for many LLM calls.
when_avoid: Avoid when solving factual questions, classification, or simple text generation where one thoughtful pass suffices, or when inference cost is tightly constrained.

faq.q: How is Tree of Thought different from chain-of-thought?
faq.a: Chain-of-thought follows one linear path and never backtracks. Tree of Thought explores multiple candidate paths in parallel, scores them, and prunes weaker branches. This adds backtracking capability at the cost of many more LLM calls.

faq.q: What does the 'score' or 'value' in Tree of Thought mean?
faq.a: It's a judgment of whether a candidate thought is on track toward the goal. You can use another LLM call to score it (e.g., "Is this step correct?"), a learned reward model, or a heuristic (e.g., "Does this get us closer to 24?").

faq.q: How much more expensive is Tree of Thought than chain-of-thought?
faq.a: Typically 10–100x more LLM calls, depending on branching factor, tree depth, and pruning aggressiveness. On the Game of 24, ToT costs ~170 calls per puzzle versus 1 for CoT, but raises solve rate from 4% to 74%.

faq.q: Can you use Tree of Thought with any language model?
faq.a: Yes, any model that can generate text and be called multiple times works. Larger models (GPT-4, Claude) tend to generate better candidates and scorers, so ToT benefits more from model strength than CoT alone does.

faq.q: Is Tree of Thought the same as beam search?
faq.a: Similar idea, different scope. Beam search is a decoding strategy at the token level (keeping top-k sequences during generation). Tree of Thought is a reasoning strategy at the semantic level (exploring candidate *thoughts* or high-level steps).

title: Text-to-Speech (TTS)
tldr: Convert written text into spoken audio using neural networks; modern models produce near-human-quality voice output.
plain_english: Text-to-speech is a system that reads text aloud as audio. Instead of a robot voice, modern TTS uses deep learning to match human speech patterns—pitch, pace, emotion, accents. You feed it text and a voice (sometimes cloned from a sample), and it outputs audio you can play immediately.
how_it_works: Modern TTS pipelines work in stages: text preprocessing tokenizes and normalizes input, a neural encoder converts tokens into acoustic features (mel-spectrograms), then a vocoder generates raw waveform audio at 16–48 kHz. End-to-end diffusion models (like F5-TTS) skip intermediate steps and learn text→speech directly. Streaming variants chunk text on-the-fly so playback starts before the full response is ready. Voice cloning fine-tunes on short speaker samples (30 seconds to 2 minutes) to adopt speaker characteristics.
why_it_matters: TTS is the voice layer for every voice agent, customer support bot, and accessibility feature. Latency—especially time-to-first-token—shapes user experience directly. Quality differences (naturalness, artifacts, emotional tone) determine whether users trust or abandon the interaction. Cloning capability unlocks brand-specific voice consistency and low-overhead localization.
example: ElevenLabs' API powers voice agents at customer service centers: a request streams text increments to TTS, audio plays within 500ms of first character arriving, and custom voice profiles ensure brand consistency across 50+ languages without hiring voice actors. A startup clones a founder's voice in 90 seconds and deploys a 24/7 sales bot that sounds like the original.
pitfalls: Streaming latency hidden in time-to-first-token: first 500ms spent buffering can feel slow even if total generation is fast.
Voice cloning on poor-quality source audio (background noise, accents inconsistent with target) produces robotic or degraded output.
Emotion/style control via prompting is unreliable across models; fine-tuning or model-specific flags work better.
Long context windows (5+ minute scripts) accumulate drift—voice characteristics shift mid-generation, especially in open-source models.
when_use: Use TTS when you need immediate, scalable voice output without hiring talent (voice agents, accessibility, localization, interactive media).
when_avoid: Avoid TTS for high-stakes human impersonation (legal/ethical red flags), broadcast-quality voice acting, or real-time latency <200ms without specialized infrastructure.

faq.q: Is TTS the same as speech synthesis?
faq.a: Yes, TTS and speech synthesis are the same thing. TTS is the modern term emphasizing neural (learning-based) synthesis; older concatenative and formant methods are now legacy.

faq.q: How long does it take TTS to generate speech?
faq.a: Time-to-first-token is typically 50–500ms depending on model and infrastructure. Full generation (streaming) happens in real-time: 30 seconds of speech takes roughly 5–15 seconds to generate, playable while generating.

faq.q: Can TTS clone a voice from a short sample?
faq.a: Yes, modern models (ElevenLabs, XTTS, F5-TTS) clone from 15–90 seconds of audio. Quality degrades with noisy source material or accents mismatched to target language.

faq.q: What's the difference between open-source and commercial TTS?
faq.a: Open-source (F5-TTS, XTTS) gives you control and no API costs but requires GPU hosting and engineering. Commercial (ElevenLabs, Cartesia) trade deployment responsibility for speed, uptime, and broader voice libraries.

faq.q: Does TTS support multiple languages and accents?
faq.a: Modern multilingual models support 50+ languages natively, but accent control within a language is limited. Voice cloning usually preserves source accent even if target language differs.

title: Underfitting
tldr: Model is too simple to learn the data's patterns, so training and test performance both stay poor.
plain_english: Underfitting happens when your model doesn't have enough capacity or flexibility to capture what's actually in the data. Think of it like trying to fit a straight line through points that form a curve—no matter how much data you feed it, the line will never match the pattern. It's the opposite problem from overfitting.
how_it_works: During training, the model receives data but lacks the architecture, feature richness, or training time to represent the underlying function. Both training loss and validation loss stay high and remain close to each other—a key diagnostic signal. Adding more data doesn't help because the model simply can't express complex patterns. Fixes include increasing model capacity (wider/deeper networks), engineering richer features, reducing regularization, or extending training.
why_it_matters: Distinguishing underfitting from overfitting changes your next action entirely. If you underfit, throwing more data at the problem wastes resources; you need a bigger or more expressive model instead. Misdiagnosing this costs time and cloud spend.
example: A small logistic regression classifier trained on customer churn data achieves 65% accuracy on both train and validation sets, showing no overfitting but poor overall performance. Adding 10× more data doesn't improve accuracy. Switching to a gradient boosted decision tree model (higher capacity) immediately raises both to 82%, revealing the original model was underfitting.
pitfalls: Confusing high training loss with overfitting—underfitting also shows high training loss, so check validation loss. If both are high and similar, you underfit.
Adding regularization (dropout, L1/L2) to an underfitting model makes it worse by reducing capacity further.
Assuming more data always helps; underfitting models ignore extra data because they lack expressiveness.
In large language models, underfitting is rare, so don't default to it as your diagnosis without checking learning curves first.
when_use: Use this diagnosis when both training and validation metrics are poor, losses are high and flat, and performance doesn't improve with more data—then increase model capacity or feature complexity.
when_avoid: Avoid this explanation if training loss is low but validation loss is high—that's overfitting, not underfitting. Also avoid it for frontier LLMs on typical tasks; they almost never underfit.

faq.q: How do I tell if my model is underfitting or overfitting?
faq.a: Plot training loss and validation loss. Both high and rising together = underfitting. Training low, validation high = overfitting. Both low = good fit.

faq.q: Will underfitting fix itself if I train longer?
faq.a: No. A model that's too simple won't improve no matter how long you train. You need to increase capacity—add layers, neurons, or better features.

faq.q: Is underfitting the same as high bias?
faq.a: Yes, in machine learning terminology. High bias means the model makes strong, oversimplified assumptions and misses the true pattern—that's underfitting.

faq.q: Can I underfit with a very large model?
faq.a: Yes, if you severely under-regularize (e.g., train for 1 epoch on massive data) or use poor features. Capacity alone doesn't guarantee good fit.

faq.q: Is underfitting common in LLMs?
faq.a: Rare in frontier models on standard tasks—they have huge capacity. Common in small task-specific classifiers and undertrained domain adapters.

title: Unsupervised Learning
tldr: Training on unlabeled data to discover patterns, clusters, or structure without explicit targets.
plain_english: Unsupervised learning finds hidden structure in data without you telling the model what to look for. Think of it like sorting a pile of unmarked photos by visual similarity without predefined categories. The model groups similar items or compresses high-dimensional data on its own, then you validate whether the groupings make sense for your problem.
how_it_works: You feed raw, unlabeled data into an algorithm. Clustering methods (k-means, DBSCAN) partition points into groups based on similarity. Dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP) projects high-dimensional data into fewer dimensions while preserving structure. Self-supervised learning derives labels from the data itself—like predicting the next word in a sentence—making it technically unsupervised from a labeling cost perspective. Modern LLMs train this way on trillions of tokens.
why_it_matters: The internet is mostly unlabeled. Self-supervised pretraining on raw text is why foundation models exist—manually labeling trillions of tokens would be impossible. You unlock value from your existing data without annotation overhead, then fine-tune on small labeled datasets if needed.
example: A streaming platform clusters user behavior (watch history, pause points, rewatches) to find audience segments without predefined genres. The algorithm discovers that users who watch slow-burn dramas also pause frequently and rewatch finales—patterns invisible to manual categorization. Validation requires domain experts checking if clusters match business intuition.
pitfalls: Cluster count is arbitrary without external validation—silhouette scores can mislead; domain grounding is required.
Self-supervised methods can memorize data structure without learning transferable representations; downstream task performance is the real test.
High-dimensional reduction (t-SNE, UMAP) distorts global structure for local clarity; interpretation requires care.
Convergence to local optima in iterative methods (k-means) means results depend on initialization.
when_use: Use unsupervised learning when you have abundant unlabeled data, need to discover hidden structure, or want to reduce dimensionality before supervised tasks.
when_avoid: Avoid unsupervised learning when you have clear labeled data and a well-defined target, or when cluster validation cannot ground results in business outcomes.

faq.q: What's the difference between unsupervised and self-supervised learning?
faq.a: Self-supervised learning derives labels from the data itself (predict next token, mask and reconstruct). Unsupervised learning has no target at all—it finds clusters or compressed representations. Self-supervised is technically unsupervised from a labeling cost perspective but trains with a defined objective.

faq.q: How do I know if my unsupervised results are any good?
faq.a: Use intrinsic metrics (silhouette score, Davies-Bouldin index) as guidance, but always validate against domain knowledge or downstream task performance. Metrics alone can be misleading; ask whether clusters make business sense.

faq.q: Is unsupervised learning the same as clustering?
faq.a: No. Clustering is one type of unsupervised learning. Others include dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE) and density estimation. Clustering specifically groups similar data points.

faq.q: Can you use unsupervised learning with an LLM?
faq.a: LLMs are trained with self-supervised learning (predicting next tokens). You can also use their embeddings for unsupervised downstream tasks like clustering documents or finding anomalies.

faq.q: Why is self-supervised pretraining better than random initialization?
faq.a: Self-supervised pretraining learns general structure from large unlabeled corpora, so fine-tuning on small labeled datasets converges faster and with less data than training from scratch.
